coredata.dc:title: Yellow emissive carbon dots with quantum yield up to 68.6% from manganese ions
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yingbo
Chao, Daiyong
Zhou, Liang
Li, Yanan
Deng, Ruiping
Zhang, Hongjie
coredata.dc:description: The development of high quantum yield (QY) carbon dots (CDs) is a key requirement for applications. However, how to engineer well-defined absorption band in visible region is a critical challenge to obtaining highly efficient long wavelength emissive CDs. In this work, the strategies of solvent-tuning absorption band and manganese ions-enhancing optical performance are introduced to prepare yellow emissive CDs. The utilization of toluene as solvent is a basis for achieving absorption band at 435 nm and realizing yellow emissive CDs with QY of 46%. Addition of manganese ions increase the degree of conjugated sp2-domians and surface states, further enhancing QY up to 68.6%. Using the yellow emissive CDs, a UV-pumped light-emitting diode (LED) was fabricated based on a 395 nm LED chip. The LED shows warm light with correlated color temperature (CCT) of 1596 K and color purity of 99%. This work provides a new insight for the development of high-quality CDs with versatile properties in pursuit of promising applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the yellow emissive carbon dots (CDs:Mn)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial aid from Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences ( Y72011 ), Research Equipment Development Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences ( YZ201562 ), Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDB20000000 ), Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Program of China ( 20170519006JH ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21771172 , 21521092 , 21590794 and 21210001 ), National Key Basic Research Program of China ( 2014CB643802 ), Jilin Province Found for Postdoctor, and the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS ( YZDY-SSW-JSC018 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram illustrates the preparation of yellow emissive carbon dots (CDs:Mn). (b) TEM, (c) size distribution and (d) AFM of the CDs:Mn. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs:Mn, (b) CDs without addition of manganese ions. Photographs of CDs:Mn and CDs in toluene solution under 395 nm hand-held lamp illumination (inset of Fig. 2a and b), (c) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs:Mn, CDs and CDs (Mn2+) and (d) time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decay curves of CDs:Mn and CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CDs prepared from (a) water, (b) ethanol, (c) N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and (d) dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XPS, (c) XRD diffraction patterns and (d) GPC curves of CDs:Mn and CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
A possible growth mechanism for (a) CDs and (b) CDs:Mn. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a)The emission spectrum the CDs:Mn based LED and (b) color coordinate of the LED. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Yellow emissive carbon dots with quantum yield up to 68.6% from manganese ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high quantum yield (QY) carbon dots (CDs) is a key requirement for applications. However, how to engineer well-defined absorption band in visible region is a critical challenge to obtaining highly efficient long wavelength emissive CDs. In this work, the strategies of solvent-tuning absorption band and manganese ions-enhancing optical performance are introduced to prepare yellow emissive CDs. The utilization of toluene as solvent is a basis for achieving absorption band at 435 nm and realizing yellow emissive CDs with QY of 46%. Addition of manganese ions increase the degree of conjugated sp2-domians and surface states, further enhancing QY up to 68.6%. Using the yellow emissive CDs, a UV-pumped light-emitting diode (LED) was fabricated based on a 395 nm LED chip. The LED shows warm light with correlated color temperature (CCT) of 1596 K and color purity of 99%. This work provides a new insight for the development of high-quality CDs with versatile properties in pursuit of promising applications.

In this work, the strategies of solvent-tuning absorption band and manganese ions-enhancing optical performance are introduced to prepare yellow emissive CDs. Addition of manganese ions increase the degree of conjugated sp2-domians and further enhance QY up to 68.6%.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), one of the most important carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, have received extensive attention due to their advantages such as tunable emission, photostability, environment friendliness and low-cost [1–3]. Owing to these merits, CDs can be used in many promising applications, such as fluorescent probes [4,5], bio-medicine [6,7] and optoelectronic devices [8,9]. Quantum yield (QY) is one of pivotal criterions to evaluate optical performance of CDs. High QY is the key requirement for implementing CDs in nearly all applications [10–12]. As a result of great studies have been devoted to improve the properties of CDs, high QYs of blue and green emissive CDs have been obtained [13,14]. In contrast, achievement of highly efficient long wavelength emissive CDs in yellow or red light region is rather challenging due to the lack of effective synthetic methods for fabricating high-quality CDs with well-defined visible absorption band. These drawbacks must greatly hamper their wide spread utilization and studies, because many applications are particularly in favorable of long wavelength emissive CDs. The yellow or red emission light is an indispensable component for generating white light in lighting devices [15,16], and efficient long wavelength emissive CDs have promising prospect for biological applications [17–20]. One of useful approaches to overcome this problem is developing a simple method for preparation of highly efficient yellow emissive carbon dots with visible excitonic absorption band.

So far, few reports have been reported for synthesizing yellow emissive carbon dots. The as-prepared CDs, one component of multicolor CDs, were synthesized from hydrothermal method or heating treatment with acid or reductant to control surface states, and subsequent separation techniques, such as silica column chromatography or molecular weight ultrafiltration, require sophisticated operations. However, the highest QY of the as-prepared CDs is 27.57% [21,22]. Although the fluorescence mechanism of the CDs is still blur, it is found that high efficient emission is directly determined by well-defined absorption peak, and emission color is determined by the absorption peak wavelength [23,24]. Similar to optical properties of the CDs with QY up to 50% [25,26], yellow emissive CDs synthesized from different organic solvents by Lin and Chen groups displayed characteristic absorption peak and an excitation-independent emission peak in the spectra, with respectable QYs of 32.2% and 46%, respectively [27,28]. However, various ingredients can hardly provide a clear guidance on how to engineer absorption band for achieving efficient yellow emissive CDs.

In this work, we demonstrate solvent-tuning absorption band strategy. We have obtained high-quality yellow emissive CDs with QY up to 46% based on toluene solvent from solvothermal treatment of citric acid and urea. Further addition of manganese ions promoted increased conjugated sp2-domains and surface states. Finally, QY of the yellow emissive CDs were further enhanced to 68.6%. Using yellow emissive CDs as a single conversion layer, we have fabricated a UV-pumped light-emitting diode (LED). The LED displayed a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 1596 K, commission internationale d’Éclairage (CIE) coordinate (0.5860, 0.4129) and color purity of 99%, which provides a potential healthy lighting for retinal protection because it staves off strong blue spike of light [29].

All chemical reagents were analytical grade without further purification. Citric acid monohydrate, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and toluene were purchased from Beijing Chemical Work. Urea and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Manganese acetate tetrahydrate, zinc acetate dihydrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Copper acetate tetrahydrate and poly (methyl methacrylate) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the yellow emissive carbon dots (CDs:Mn)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 1 mmol citric acid, 2 mmol urea and 1 mmol manganese acetate were dispersed in 12.5 mL toluene. Then, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the raw samples were purified by centrifugation at 10000 r/min for 25 min, then the collected solution was further filtered using 0.22 μM filter member.

Yellow emissive CDs:Mn in toluene solution were mixed with poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) by vigorously stirring until the PMMA was totally soluble to form homogeneous solution. The solvent was vaporized under ambient conditions to form sticky CDs/PMMA composites. Then, the composites were deposited on a UV-chip with 395 nm excitation light to fabricate LED devices.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were record using FEI Tecnai G2F20 TEM. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed on malvern nano-ZS90. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) measurements were carried out using D8 FOCUS power X-ray diffractometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed on a Bruker MultiMode 8 instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi instrument. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using FLSP-920 steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectrometer. Molecular weight (MW) was measured by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) method using tetrahydrofuran (THF) as solvent on a Waters 1525 instrument. Absolute quantum yields of the samples were measured in a calibrated integrating sphere on a C-9920-02 instrument. The LED parameters were measured on a SSP6612 photometric colormetric electric tester.

The as-prepared CDs from solvothermal treatment of citric acid, urea and manganese acetate is denoted as CDs:Mn (Fig. 1 a). Subsequently, the morphologies of the CDs:Mn were analyzed. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CDs:Mn reveals they are uniform and narrowly distributed particles with an average diameter of about 2.95 nm (Fig. 1b and c), which is also confirmed by dynamic light scatting (DLS) analysis (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 1d, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of the CDs:Mn reveals their topographic heights in the range about 1.0–2.0 nm, corresponding to three to six layers of graphenen-like sheets in the CDs:Mn [30].

To better understand the effect of manganese (Mn2+) ions on optical property of the CDs:Mn, CDs without Mn2+ ions were prepared by the same approach. As shown in Fig. S2, the pure CDs are well-dispersed with average particle size of 2.68 nm which is smaller than that of CDs:Mn of 2.95 nm. As shown in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2 a and b), both CDs:Mn and CDs exhibit excitation-wavelength-independent emission while shifting excitation wavelengths from 350 to 450 nm, indicating the fluorescence behaviors originate from homogeneous emissive states [31]. The maximum emission peak of the CDs was centered at 550 nm, whereas addition of Mn2+ ions caused the as-prepared CDs:Mn show red-shifted emission peak centered at 555 nm. The CDs:Mn exhibit deeper and more bright yellow fluorescence than that of the CDs in dilute toluene solution under 395 nm hand-held UV lamp illumination (insets of Fig. 2a and b), which ties in well with the emissive features of the both samples. As shown in Fig. 2a and b, it is interesting to find that fluorescence spectra of the both samples show relatively weak emission peak centered at 450 nm, which is excited in wide wavelengths from 350 to 400 nm. This result is further confirmed by a relatively weak absorption peak at 318 nm in fluorescence excitation spectra (FLE) of the both samples (Fig. S3). The origin fluorescence features of the both samples will be discussed in detail below. The UV–via absorption spectra of the both samples, as presented in Fig. 2c, show broad absorption bands covering UV and visible light regions. Absorption bands centered at 435 and 450 nm correspond to CDs and CDs:Mn, respectively, which can be ascribed to n→π* transitions from highly conjugated domains containing C=O/C-O groups [32,33]. In comparison with CDs, CDs:Mn show broader absorption band and red-shifted emission peak, suggesting that Mn2+ ions could enlarge conjugated sp2-domains, thus lowering the band gap of the as-prepared CDs:Mn (Fig. 2c) [34]. In order to elucidate effects of interactions between Mn2+ ions and pure CDs, the pure CDs solution mixed with Mn2+ ions was denoted as CDs (Mn2+). As shown in Fig. 2c, absorption band and fluorescence emission peak of CDs (Mn2+) almost overlap with that of CDs, indicating optical behaviors of CDs:Mn should be not mainly from the interactions between pure CDs and Mn2+ ions. As shown in Fig. S4, the absolute QY of CDs without Mn2+ ions is 46%, on the other hand, QY of CDs:Mn can be further enhanced up to 68.6% due to synergistic effect of increased conjugated sp2-domain and minimal increase nitrogen and oxygen content as discussed below [32,35]. The photoluminescence (PL) decay curves of both samples are fitted to two-exponential (Fig. 2d). The fast (τ1) and low (τ2) components for both samples are on a nanosecond time scale, indicating both samples possess fluorescent properties (Table S1) [36]. As shown in Table S1, the percentages of the low component (τ2) are 73.71% and 89.66% for CDs and CDs:Mn, respectively, indicating it is mainly responsible for yellow emissive process. The lifetime of CDs is 8.11 ns, upon addition of Mn2+ ions, the increased percentage of τ2 results in a longer lifetime of 8.75 ns, which is conductive to efficient fluorescence emission. Both CDs:Mn and CDs are highly stable under continuous excitation wavelengths at 360 nm and 450 nm for an hour (Fig. S5). Moreover, both CDs:Mn and CDs are well-dispersed in toluene after 5 months, and no changes in peak characteristics of absorption and fluorescence spectra are observed (Fig. S6).

Furthermore, to investigate the influence of solvent on the optical properties of as-prepared CDs, some other solvents including water, ethanol, DMF and DMSO were also utilized and compared in the experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 , the CDs prepared from different solvents exhibit tunable absorption bands with long wavelength centered at 334, 434, 501, and 504 nm for the CDs synthesized from water, ethanol, DMF and DMSO, respectively. Accordingly, the corresponding emission spectra display distinguishable fluorescence behaviors with maximum peak wavelengths at 450, 550, 543, and 548 nm, respectively. Based on these results, it can be concluded that solvent plays a key role for adjusting absorption bands, consequently obtaining various fluorescence behaviors. The possible reason underlying the results is that solvents can affect the degree of dehydration and carbonization process, which result in formation of different sized of sp2-conjugated domains [37]. Herein, the use of toluene is a key factor for engineering absorption bands with excitonic absorption peak at 435 nm, thus realizing yellow emissive carbon dots, while addition of Mn2+ ions is an efficient strategy in enhancing luminescent efficiency of the yellow emissive CDs:Mn. The weak emission peak at 450 nm in both samples is probably due to the existence of another emissive state caused by the mixture of trace amount of water from precursors, which is further confirmed by the fact that increasing amount of water in reaction system lead to increased emission intensity at 450 nm for the obtained CDs (Fig. S7).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were used to investigate their surface groups. As shown in Fig. 4 a, both samples exhibit highly similar FT-IR spectra, indicating the existence of similar surface chemical groups. In FT-IR spectra, stretching vibrations of O-H (∼3640 cm−1), N-H (∼3200 cm−1), C-O (∼1240 cm−1) and COOH (1715 cm−1) are observed. Absorption bands peaked at 1379 and 1466 cm−1 are attributed to C-H and C=C vibrations form polyaromatic structures, respectively, indicating the presence of conjugated sp2-domains. The surface states of both samples were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron energy spectroscopy (XPS). The full survey spectra, as presented in Fig. 4b, show peaks of C 1s (285.20 eV), N 1s (420.9 eV) and O 1s (532.5 eV) for the CDs:Mn. But only two peaks of C 1s (283.7 eV) and O 1s (532.5 eV) are observed for the CDs may due to inadequate nitrogen content in the CDs. However, the precursor, urea, plays an important role in determining fluorescence behaviors as verified by the distinct excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of the CDs prepared only from citric acid (CA). As shown in Fig. S8, emission peak of the CDs prepared from CA shifts from 522 to 542 nm with alternating excitation wavelengths from 350 to 450 nm. The element composition ratio of C/N/O is 0.9393/0.0033/0.0573 for CDs:Mn, while CDs possess the C/O element ratio of 0.9566/0.0434. The atomic ratios between carbon and oxygen are about 16/1 and 22/1 for CDs:Mn and CDs, respectively, which are greatly larger than that of 1/1 about citric acid and urea, indicating deoxygenation reactions occurs during solvothermal process [38]. The high-resolution C1s spectra for the both samples are almost dominated by one peak of C-C/C=C, indicating high percentage of carbon atoms for the both samples (Fig. S9). The high-resolution O1s spectrum of the CDs:Mn can be deconvoluted to two peaks at 531.8 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to C=O (eV) and C-O (eV) groups, respectively (Fig. S10a). The deconvoluted peaks of the C=O (531.8 eV) and C-O (533.03 eV) groups are observed for the CDs (Fig. S10b). As shown in Fig. 4c, the both samples exhibit highly similar X-ray diffraction patterns. The diffraction peaks at 13.87 and 16.90°, may correspond to (002) facet of graphene, indicating partial graphitization of the both samples, while the diffraction peak at 20.10 and 42.74° refer to amorphous carbon and (101) planes of graphene [39,40]. As revealed by the above results, the surface states and structure of CDs:Mn and CDs are closely alike. Furthermore, the molecular weight of the sample is analyzed by gel permeation chromatography (GPC). The average molecular weight for the CDs is 6852, which is lower than that of the CDs:Mn (7289) (Fig. 4d).

Based on above results about relationship between chemical structure/compositions and optical properties of both samples, it can be concluded that: in comparison with pure CDs, larger particle size and molecular weight are observed for CDs:Mn (Fig. 1, Fig. S2 and Fig. 4d), accompanied by red-shifted absorbance band and emission peak wavelength (Fig. 2c). These results suggest that CDs:Mn have an increase extent of conjugated sp2-doamins. Additionally, from XPS results (Fig .4b), no manganese element is observed, and minimal increase of nitrogen and oxygen content for CDs:Mn. Thus, a possible mechanism is proposed as briefly illustrated in Fig. 5 . In the mixed solution, the metal (Mn2+) ions attract more citric acid molecules through inter-molecular coordination bonding. During the course of solvothermal conditions, more citric acid molecules should assemble larger molecular with more surface groups at edge sites [41], then react with urea and finally convert to the CDs:Mn with larger conjugated sp2-domains and more surface groups. To further confirm this mechanism, other metal (Zn2+ and Cu2+) ions were performed. As shown in Fig. S11a, the absorption peak wavelength (450 nm) of the CDs:Zn is in good agreement with the corresponding absorption peak of the CDs:Mn. The emission spectra of the CDs:Zn are very similar to that of CDs:Mn, with excitation independent emission peak at 562 nm (Fig. S11b). These results indicate that Zn2+ ions have the same effect as the Mn2+. However, Cu2+ ions do not work well as demonstrated by its absorption and fluorescence spectra. Although absorption spectrum of the CDs:Cu exhibits absorption band at 435 nm (Fig. S12a), its fluorescence behavior is different. The emission peak of the CDs:Cu at 450 nm is the dominant with excitation wavelengths from 350 to 390 nm, which is stronger than the emission peak at 541 nm (Fig. S12b). Herein, the interactions between metal ions and pure CDs were considered. As shown in Fig. S13, the peak characteristics of CDs:Zn and CDs:Cu are different from that of CDs (Zn2+) and CDs (Cu2+), which were obtained by mixing Zn2+ and Cu2+ ions with pure CDs solution, respectively, implying optical behaviors of CDs:Zn and CDs:Cu should be not mainly from interactions between metal ions and pure CDs. In this mechanism, metal ions would show different activity due to their differences, such as solubility, complexing ability, electronic structure. In light of reactivity difference and ambiguous solvothermal process, more works are needed to fully understand the obtained performance and verify the proposed mechanism.

As direct merit of highly efficient yellow emission, the lighting device was fabricated based on a 395 nm UV-LED chip. Then, the CDs:Mn dispersed in poly (methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) as conversion layer was deposited on UV-LED chip. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the electroluminescence spectrum of the LED shows emission spectrum ranging from 530 to 780 nm with emission peak at 598 nm. In the inset of Fig. 6a, the LED exhibits warm orange emissive color at an electric voltage drive. Meanwhile, the LED can realize warm light with CIE coordinates of (0.5860, 0.4129), CCT of 1596 K and color purity of 99% (Fig. 6b). It is suggested that the obtained warm LED is suitable for indoor lighting due to its comfortable lighting, which can stave off blue light injury.

In conclusion, it has been demonstrated that solvent is a key factor for tuning absorption bands, and consequently obtaining various fluorescence behaviors of the synthesized CDs. Herein, toluene is a basis for obtaining absorption band at 435 nm, and further realizing yellow emissive CDs with QY of 46%. Addition of Mn2+ ions enhanced QY up to 68.6%. A possible mechanism was proposed that metal ions could enable polymerization of more molecules under solvothermal conditions, ensuring a high degree of conjugated sp2-domains and increased surface states. A UV-pumped LED was fabricated based on yellow emissive CDs:Mn in combination with a 395 nm LED chip. The LED showed warm light with CIE coordinates of (0.5860, 0.4129), CCT of 1596 K and color purity of 99%. In this work, it is anticipated the solvent-tuning and metal ions-enhancing synthetic strategy will promote the development of highly efficient CDs with versatile properties in pursuit of promising applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial aid from Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Y72011), Research Equipment Development Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YZ201562), Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDB20000000), Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Program of China (20170519006JH), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21771172, 21521092, 21590794 and 21210001), National Key Basic Research Program of China (2014CB643802), Jilin Province Found for Postdoctor, and the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (YZDY-SSW-JSC018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.02.004.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
